text: This video will demonstrate a robotic right middle lobectomy in a young patient with a 2.5 centimeter cancer of the right middle lobe.
The dissection is started by mobilizing the inferior pulmonary ligament as seen here, all the way up to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.
And in doing so, we encounter this black structure, which is a level nine lymph node.
This is removed and will be sent off for testing.
Once this is done, the lung is retracted anteriorly to expose the posterior hilum, specifically the subcrinal space.
We incise a pleura over the posterior hilum to expose these level seven lymph nodes, and each individual lymph node is removed and sent for pathology and testing.
Here you can see the subcrinal space completely cleaned of all lymph node tissue.
And once this is done, the lung is folded down towards the diaphragm to expose the peritracheal space where level 4R lymph nodes and level 2R lymph nodes are removed.
The lung is then folded posteriorly to expose the anterior hilum.
We'll start the dissection by identifying the middle lobe vein as seen here.
Here you'll see the crotch between the middle lobe vein and the upper lobe vein where a lymph node can be seen.
This lymph node is then removed to help facilitate better dissection.
Once the lymph node is removed, we're able to get around the middle lobe vein, and it is then transected with the vascular load of the stapler.
We next turn our attention to identifying the middle lobe bronchus, where again we're encountered with a lymph node there, and this lymph node will be removed completely as well.
The pearly white structure that's coming into view is the ongoing pulmonary artery to the lower lobe.
You'll see that with the dissection and the robot, you're able to fairly easily remove the entire lymph node without creating much bleeding.
And removing all of the lymph node tissue really helps with the complete dissection and helps get around each of these structures more easily.
And here again is the middle lobe bronchus and the cardiac grasp are coming around it.
And once we're able to do that, a green load of the stapler is used to transect the airway.
Next we'll identify the pulmonary artery going to the middle lobe.
There are typically two branches of the pulmonary artery.
This is the first of the two.
And once we're again around this vessel completely, we will transect it with the vascular load of the stapler.
And here on screen right is the superior pulmonary lobe vein.
And on screen left is the artery going to the middle lobe.
The second of the two branches going to the middle lobe.
And once we are completely around this vessel, two will be transected with the vascular lobe of the stapler.
And lastly, the fissure connecting the upper lobe and the middle lobe is transected using green load to the stapler.
And once this is done, the middle lobe will be completely separated from the right lung, completing the lobectomy.
We do perform an intercostal nerve block for post-operative pain control.
Progel is placed on the remaining lung and suture lines to help prevent air leaks.
The specimen is placed in a bag and retrieved through the anterior most incision.
This patient did very well postoperatively and was discharged on day one after surgery.
A chest tube is placed and the lung is then reinflated.
